rawText:
So I want to start by offering you a free no-tech life hack, and all it requires of you is this: that you change your posture for two minutes. But before I give it away, I want to ask you to right now do a little audit of your body and what you're doing with your body. So how many of you are sort of making yourselves smaller? Maybe you're hunching, crossing your legs, maybe wrapping your ankles. Sometimes we hold onto our arms like this. Sometimes we spread out.

I see you. So I want you to pay attention to what you're doing right now. We're going to come back to that in a few minutes, and I'm hoping that if you learn to tweak this a little bit, it could significantly change the way your life unfolds.

So, we're really fascinated with body language, and we're particularly interested in other people's body language. You know, we're interested in, like, you know -- an awkward interaction, or a smile, or a contemptuous glance, or maybe a very awkward wink, or maybe even something like a handshake.

So obviously when we think about nonverbal behavior, or body language -- but we call it nonverbals as social scientists -- it's language, so we think about communication. When we think about communication, we think about interactions. So what is your body language communicating to me? What's mine communicating to you?

And there's a lot of reason to believe that this is a valid way to look at this. So social scientists have spent a lot of time looking at the effects of our body language, or other people's body language, on judgments. And we make sweeping judgments and inferences from body language. And those judgments can predict really meaningful life outcomes like who we hire or promote, who we ask out on a date.

For example, Nalini Ambady, a researcher at Tufts University, shows that when people watch 30-second soundless clips of real physician-patient interactions, their judgments of the physician's niceness predict whether or not that physician will be sued. So it doesn't have to do so much with whether or not that physician was incompetent, but do we like that person and how they interacted?

Even more dramatic, Alex Todorov at Princeton has shown us that judgments of political candidates' faces in just one second predict 70 percent of U.S. Senate and gubernatorial race outcomes, and even, let's go digital, emoticons used well in online negotiations can lead you to claim more value from that negotiation. If you use them poorly, bad idea. Right?

So when we think of nonverbals, we think of how we judge others, how they judge us and what the outcomes are. We tend to forget, though, the other audience that's influenced by our nonverbals, and that's ourselves. We are also influenced by our nonverbals, our thoughts and our feelings and our physiology.

So what nonverbals am I talking about? I'm a social psychologist. I study prejudice, and I teach at a competitive business school, so it was inevitable that I would become interested in power dynamics. I became especially interested in nonverbal expressions of power and dominance.

overview.title: Your Body Language May Shape Who You Are

sentences.original: So I want to start by offering you a free no-tech life hack, and all it requires of you is this: that you change your posture for two minutes.
sentences.translation: 저는 무료이면서 기술이 필요 없는 생활 꿀팁 하나를 알려드리며 시작하고 싶습니다. 여러분에게 필요한 건 단 하나, 2분 동안 자세를 바꾸는 것뿐입니다.

grammarElements.text: by offering you a free no-tech life hack
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (수단)

grammarElements.text: and all it requires of you is this
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (접속사 and)

grammarElements.text: that you change your posture for two minutes
grammarElements.label: 동격절 (that절 — this의 내용)

vocabulary.example: Good posture can prevent back pain.
This simple life hack will save you hours every week.
This is a no-tech solution to a modern problem.

expressions.usage: 프레젠테이션이나 이야기를 특정 행동으로 시작할 때 사용

expressions.phrase: all it requires of you is
expressions.usage: 필요 조건이 간단함을 강조할 때 사용

patterns.pattern: I want to start by [verb]-ing ...
patterns.explanation: 발표나 대화를 시작할 때 첫 행동을 소개하는 패턴. TED 강연에서 매우 자주 사용된다.
examples: I want to start by sharing a personal story.
Let me start by thanking everyone here.

drillQuestions.question: I want to start _____ offering you some advice.
All it _____ of you is two minutes of your time.

sentences.notes: TED 강연의 전형적인 오프닝. "life hack"은 실리콘밸리 문화에서 유래한 표현으로, 간단한 해결책을 의미한다. that절은 콜론(:) 뒤에 나오는 this의 동격절이다.

koreanAlignment.ko: 무료이면서 기술 필요 없는 생활 꿀팁을

koreanAlignment.en: and all it requires of you is this

pronunciationNotes: posture: /ˈpɑːstʃər/ — "파스처", ch 발음이 아닌 "츄" 소리에 유의
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 직접 호소(direct appeal) — 청중에게 바로 말을 걸어 관심을 유도하는 기법

sentences.original: But before I give it away, I want to ask you to right now do a little audit of your body and what you're doing with your body.
sentences.translation: 하지만 그것을 알려드리기 전에, 지금 바로 여러분의 몸과 몸으로 무엇을 하고 있는지 간단히 점검해 보시길 부탁드립니다.

grammarElements.text: to right now do a little audit of your body
grammarElements.label: 목적격 보어 (to부정사)

grammarElements.text: and what you're doing with your body
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (and + 간접의문문)

vocabulary.example: We need to do a quick audit of our expenses.
I don't want to give away the ending of the movie.

expressions.usage: 곧 공개할 정보에 대한 기대를 높일 때 사용하는 표현
비즈니스 용어를 일상 맥락에서 가볍게 사용

patterns.pattern: Before [subject] [verb], [subject] want to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 종속 접속사 before로 시간 순서를 나타내는 복문 구조. 먼저 할 일을 제시한 후 본론으로 넘어간다.
examples: Before we begin, I want to introduce our guest.
Before I explain the results, I want to show you the data.

drillQuestions.question: _____ I give it away, I want to ask you something.
I want to ask you _____ do a little audit.

sentences.notes: audit는 원래 회계/감사 용어이지만, 여기서는 비유적으로 "자기 몸 상태를 점검하다"라는 의미로 사용. 일상 영어에서 전문용어를 비유적으로 쓰는 예시이다.

koreanAlignment.en: But before I give it away
to right now do a little audit
and what you're doing with your body

pronunciationNotes: right now: 강연에서는 "라잇 나우"를 빠르게 연결하여 "라잇나우"처럼 발음

sentences.original: So how many of you are sort of making yourselves smaller? Maybe you're hunching, crossing your legs, maybe wrapping your ankles.
sentences.translation: 여러분 중 얼마나 많은 분이 자신을 작게 만들고 계신가요? 아마 등을 구부리거나, 다리를 꼬거나, 발목을 감고 계실 수도 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: Maybe you're hunching, crossing your legs, maybe wrapping your ankles
grammarElements.label: 병렬구조 (현재분사 나열)

vocabulary.example: She hunched over her desk while typing.
She wrapped her arms around herself.
I'm sort of tired after the long meeting.

expressions.usage: 청중에게 참여를 유도하는 수사적 질문
몸을 움츠리는 행동을 비유적으로 표현

patterns.pattern: How many of you are [verb]-ing ...?
patterns.explanation: 청중의 참여를 끌어내는 수사적 질문 패턴. TED 강연과 프레젠테이션에서 자주 사용.
examples: How many of you are feeling nervous right now?
How many of you are thinking about your phones?

drillQuestions.question: How many of you _____ sort of making yourselves smaller?
Maybe you're hunching, _____ your legs.

sentences.notes: sort of는 구어체에서 단정을 피하는 완화 표현(hedge). make + 목적어 + 형용사(smaller)는 5형식 구조. 동사 나열(hunching, crossing, wrapping)은 병렬구조로 같은 동작 유형을 연속 제시한다.

koreanAlignment.en: are sort of making yourselves smaller

pronunciationNotes: ankles: /ˈæŋklz/ — "앵클즈", ng 발음 후 k가 이어짐
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수사적 질문(rhetorical question) — 대답을 기대하기보다 청중이 스스로 상황을 인식하도록 유도

sentences.original: Sometimes we hold onto our arms like this. Sometimes we spread out.
sentences.translation: 때때로 우리는 이렇게 팔을 잡고 있기도 합니다. 때로는 몸을 쫙 펼치기도 하죠.

vocabulary.example: Hold onto the railing when going downstairs.
She spread out the map on the table.

expressions.usage: 시각적 시연과 함께 사용하여 동작을 보여줄 때

patterns.pattern: Sometimes we [verb] ... Sometimes we [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 대조적인 행동을 병렬로 제시하는 패턴. 반복 구조가 리듬감을 준다.
examples: Sometimes we laugh. Sometimes we cry.
Sometimes we succeed. Sometimes we fail.

drillQuestions.question: Sometimes we hold _____ our arms like this.

sentences.notes: hold onto와 spread out은 정반대의 신체 동작을 표현하는 구동사. 짧은 문장 두 개의 대비가 몸을 움츠리는 것과 펼치는 것의 차이를 시각적으로 보여준다.
pronunciationNotes: hold onto: "홀드 온투" — 연음으로 "홀돈투"처럼 빠르게 발음될 수 있음
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대구법(antithesis) — 작아지는 동작(hold onto)과 펼치는 동작(spread out)을 대비

sentences.original: I see you. So I want you to pay attention to what you're doing right now.
sentences.translation: 보입니다. 그러니 지금 여러분이 무엇을 하고 있는지 주의를 기울여 주세요.

grammarElements.text: to what you're doing right now
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (간접의문문 포함)

vocabulary.example: Please pay attention to the safety instructions.

expressions.usage: 특정 대상에 집중하도록 요청할 때

patterns.pattern: I want you to [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 상대방에게 행동을 요청하는 5형식 패턴. want + 목적어 + to부정사 구조.
examples: I want you to listen carefully.
I want you to think about this.

drillQuestions.question: I want you _____ pay attention to what you are doing.
Pay attention _____ what you are doing right now.

sentences.notes: "I see you"는 짧지만 강렬한 연결 문장으로, 청중과의 유대감을 형성한다. what절은 간접의문문으로 pay attention to의 목적어 역할을 한다.

koreanAlignment.en: So I want you to pay attention

sentences.original: We're going to come back to that in a few minutes, and I'm hoping that if you learn to tweak this a little bit, it could significantly change the way your life unfolds.
sentences.translation: 몇 분 후에 다시 이 얘기로 돌아올 겁니다. 그리고 이것을 약간만 조정하는 법을 배우면, 여러분 인생이 펼쳐지는 방식이 크게 달라질 수 있기를 바랍니다.

grammarElements.text: We're going to come back to that
grammarElements.label: 주절 (be going to 미래)

grammarElements.text: that if you learn to tweak this a little bit
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (that + if 조건절)

grammarElements.text: it could significantly change the way your life unfolds
grammarElements.label: 주절 (조건문의 결과절)

vocabulary.example: We need to tweak the design a little bit.
The story unfolds over several decades.
The results have significantly improved.

expressions.usage: 이전 화제로 다시 돌아갈 것을 예고할 때 사용
삶의 전개 과정을 은유적으로 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: If you learn to [verb], it could [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 조건문 구조. 조건을 제시하고 가능한 결과를 설명한다. could는 가능성을 나타냄.
examples: If you learn to manage your time, it could improve your productivity.
If you learn to listen, it could strengthen your relationships.

drillQuestions.question: If you learn to _____ this a little bit, it could change your life.
It could significantly change the way your life _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 강연의 핵심 약속(promise)을 담고 있다. "2분의 자세 변화가 인생을 바꿀 수 있다"는 메시지. that절 안에 if 조건절이 내포된 복합 구조이다.

koreanAlignment.en: We're going to come back to that
if you learn to tweak this a little bit

sentences.original: So, we're really fascinated with body language, and we're particularly interested in other people's body language.
sentences.translation: 우리는 보디랭귀지에 정말 매료되어 있습니다. 그리고 특히 다른 사람들의 보디랭귀지에 관심이 있죠.

vocabulary.example: I'm fascinated by ancient history.
I'm particularly interested in marine biology.

expressions.usage: 어떤 주제에 깊은 관심과 흥미를 느낄 때
특정 분야에 흥미를 보일 때 가장 기본적인 표현

patterns.pattern: We're [adjective] with/in [topic], and we're particularly [adjective] in [specific topic]
patterns.explanation: 일반적 관심에서 구체적 관심으로 좁혀가는 패턴. particularly로 강조를 더한다.
examples: We're excited about the project, and we're particularly focused on the design.
I'm interested in science, and I'm particularly drawn to physics.

drillQuestions.question: We're really fascinated _____ body language.
We're _____ interested in other people's body language.

sentences.notes: fascinated with와 interested in은 전치사가 다르므로 주의. 감정 형용사(fascinated, interested)는 주로 수동태 형태로 사용되며 전치사와 세트로 외워야 한다.

sentences.original: You know, we're interested in, like, you know -- an awkward interaction, or a smile, or a contemptuous glance, or maybe a very awkward wink, or maybe even something like a handshake.
sentences.translation: 있잖아요, 우리는 어색한 상호작용이나, 미소, 경멸적인 눈길, 아주 어색한 윙크, 혹은 악수 같은 것에 관심이 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: an awkward interaction, or a smile, or a contemptuous glance, or maybe a very awkward wink, or maybe even something like a handshake
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (or로 연결된 병렬 나열)

vocabulary.example: There was an awkward silence after his joke.
She gave him a contemptuous look.
She gave a quick glance at her phone.
He gave her a friendly wink.

expressions.usage: 구어체에서 말의 공백을 채우거나 청자의 동의를 구할 때
정확한 예시보다 대략적인 예시를 들 때

patterns.pattern: [A], or [B], or [C], or maybe even [D]
patterns.explanation: or로 여러 예시를 나열하면서 점점 범위를 넓히는 패턴. maybe even으로 극적인 예시를 추가.
examples: It could be a word, or a gesture, or maybe even a look.
You might feel happy, or excited, or maybe even overwhelmed.

drillQuestions.question: An _____ interaction can make everyone uncomfortable.

sentences.notes: "you know"와 "like"는 영어 구어체의 대표적인 filler(삽입어)로, TED 강연처럼 준비된 발표에서도 자연스러움을 주기 위해 사용한다. contemptuous는 고급 어휘로 주의 필요.

koreanAlignment.en: or maybe a very awkward wink
or maybe even something like a handshake

sentences.original: So obviously when we think about nonverbal behavior, or body language -- but we call it nonverbals as social scientists -- it's language, so we think about communication.
sentences.translation: 그러니까 분명히 비언어적 행동, 즉 보디랭귀지를 생각할 때 — 사회과학자로서 우리는 이것을 비언어(nonverbals)라고 부릅니다 — 그것은 언어이므로, 우리는 의사소통을 생각하게 됩니다.

grammarElements.text: when we think about nonverbal behavior
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — when)

grammarElements.text: but we call it nonverbals as social scientists
grammarElements.label: 삽입절 (대시 사이)

vocabulary.example: Nonverbal cues are important in communication.
She was obviously tired after the long trip.

expressions.phrase: we call it X as social scientists
expressions.usage: 전문 용어를 소개할 때 직업적 관점을 밝히며 사용

patterns.pattern: When we think about [X], we think about [Y]
patterns.explanation: 하나의 개념에서 관련 개념으로 자연스럽게 연결하는 논리 전개 패턴.
examples: When we think about health, we think about diet and exercise.
When we think about success, we think about hard work.

drillQuestions.question: When we think about communication, we think about _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 여러 절이 대시(--), 접속사(but, so)로 연결된 복잡한 구조. 구어체의 자유로운 흐름을 반영한다. nonverbals는 학술 용어를 복수형 명사로 사용한 예.

koreanAlignment.en: when we think about nonverbal behavior

sentences.original: When we think about communication, we think about interactions. So what is your body language communicating to me? What's mine communicating to you?
sentences.translation: 의사소통을 생각하면, 상호작용을 떠올리게 됩니다. 그렇다면 여러분의 보디랭귀지는 저에게 무엇을 전달하고 있나요? 제 보디랭귀지는 여러분에게 무엇을 전달하고 있나요?

grammarElements.text: what is your body language communicating to me
grammarElements.label: 의문문 (현재진행형)

vocabulary.example: Social interaction is essential for mental health.
Body language communicates more than words.

expressions.meaning: 제 것은 당신에게 무엇을 전달하고 있나요?
expressions.usage: 소유대명사 mine을 사용하여 반복을 피하는 자연스러운 표현

patterns.pattern: What is [X] communicating to [Y]?
patterns.explanation: 비언어적 신호가 전달하는 메시지를 질문하는 패턴. 의인화 표현.
examples: What is your silence communicating to others?
What is this data communicating to researchers?

drillQuestions.question: What is your body language _____ to me?

sentences.notes: mine은 소유대명사로 "my body language"의 반복을 피함. 두 질문의 대칭 구조(to me / to you)가 쌍방향 소통을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: what is your body language communicating to me

sentences.original: And there's a lot of reason to believe that this is a valid way to look at this.
sentences.translation: 그리고 이것이 타당한 접근 방식이라고 믿을 만한 이유가 많습니다.

grammarElements.text: that this is a valid way to look at this
grammarElements.label: that절 (believe의 목적어)

vocabulary.example: That is a valid point.

expressions.phrase: there's a lot of reason to believe
expressions.usage: 주장의 근거가 있음을 서론적으로 제시할 때

expressions.phrase: a valid way to look at
expressions.usage: 특정 관점의 정당성을 주장할 때

patterns.pattern: There's a lot of reason to [verb] that ...
patterns.explanation: 어떤 주장이나 믿음에 대한 근거가 충분함을 나타내는 학술적 표현 패턴.
examples: There's a lot of reason to believe that exercise improves mood.
There's a lot of reason to think that this approach works.

drillQuestions.question: There's a lot of reason to _____ that this is valid.

sentences.notes: this is a valid way to look at this에서 두 개의 this가 각각 다른 대상을 가리킨다. 첫 번째 this는 "보디랭귀지 연구", 두 번째 this는 "의사소통/판단"이다.

koreanAlignment.en: that this is a valid way

sentences.original: So social scientists have spent a lot of time looking at the effects of our body language, or other people's body language, on judgments.
sentences.translation: 그래서 사회과학자들은 우리의 보디랭귀지, 또는 다른 사람들의 보디랭귀지가 판단에 미치는 영향을 연구하는 데 많은 시간을 보냈습니다.

grammarElements.text: of our body language, or other people's body language
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (of)

vocabulary.example: The effect of the medicine was immediate.
We should avoid making snap judgments.

expressions.usage: 어떤 활동에 오래 시간을 투입했음을 표현

expressions.phrase: the effects of X on Y
expressions.usage: 원인과 결과의 관계를 학술적으로 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] have spent a lot of time [verb]-ing ...
patterns.explanation: 현재완료 + spend time -ing 패턴. 오랫동안 지속된 활동을 나타낸다.
examples: Researchers have spent a lot of time studying climate change.
I have spent a lot of time thinking about this problem.

drillQuestions.question: Scientists have spent a lot of time _____ at the data.
The effects of body language _____ judgments are significant.

sentences.notes: spend + time + -ing은 영어의 핵심 패턴 중 하나. effect on과 effect of의 전치사 차이에 주의: of 뒤에는 원인, on 뒤에는 영향을 받는 대상이 온다.

koreanAlignment.en: have spent a lot of time

sentences.original: And we make sweeping judgments and inferences from body language. And those judgments can predict really meaningful life outcomes like who we hire or promote, who we ask out on a date.
sentences.translation: 우리는 보디랭귀지로부터 광범위한 판단과 추론을 합니다. 그리고 그 판단들은 누구를 고용하거나 승진시킬지, 누구에게 데이트 신청을 할지와 같은 정말 의미 있는 인생의 결과를 예측할 수 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: like who we hire or promote, who we ask out on a date
grammarElements.label: 예시 (like + 관계대명사절)

vocabulary.example: The government made sweeping changes to the law.
We can make inferences from the available data.
Scientists predict that temperatures will rise.
She was promoted to senior manager.

expressions.usage: 제한된 정보로 큰 결론을 내릴 때 (때로 부정적 뉘앙스)
누군가에게 만남을 제안할 때 사용하는 일상 표현

patterns.pattern: [Judgments] can predict [outcomes] like [examples]
patterns.explanation: 판단이 결과를 예측한다는 인과관계를 제시하고, like 이후에 구체적 예시를 나열.
examples: First impressions can predict outcomes like job success or relationship quality.
Test scores can predict outcomes like college admission.

drillQuestions.question: We make _____ judgments from body language.
Those judgments can _____ really meaningful life outcomes.

sentences.notes: sweeping은 "빗자루로 쓸 듯이 넓게"라는 비유에서 나온 표현. like 뒤에 who 관계대명사절이 나와 구체적 예시를 든다. 일상(데이트)과 직장(고용, 승진)을 병치하여 보디랭귀지의 광범위한 영향을 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: we make sweeping judgments and inferences
like who we hire or promote
who we ask out on a date

sentences.original: For example, Nalini Ambady, a researcher at Tufts University, shows that when people watch 30-second soundless clips of real physician-patient interactions, their judgments of the physician's niceness predict whether or not that physician will be sued.
sentences.translation: 예를 들어, 터프츠 대학교의 연구자인 날리니 앰버디는, 사람들이 실제 의사-환자 상호작용의 30초짜리 무음 영상을 볼 때, 의사의 친절함에 대한 판단이 그 의사가 소송을 당할지 여부를 예측한다는 것을 보여줍니다.

grammarElements.text: Nalini Ambady, a researcher at Tufts University
grammarElements.label: 주어 + 동격 (설명)

grammarElements.text: when people watch 30-second soundless clips of real physician-patient interactions
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — when)

grammarElements.text: their judgments of the physician's niceness
grammarElements.label: that절의 주어

grammarElements.text: whether or not that physician will be sued
grammarElements.label: 명사절 (whether — predict의 목적어)

vocabulary.example: The video was soundless but still powerful.
You should consult a physician about your symptoms.
Her niceness was appreciated by everyone.
The company was sued for negligence.

expressions.usage: 두 가지 가능성을 모두 포함하는 간접 의문을 표현

patterns.pattern: [Research] shows that when [condition], [result]
patterns.explanation: 연구 결과를 소개하는 학술적 패턴. when절은 조건, 주절은 결과를 보여준다.
examples: Research shows that when people exercise regularly, their mood improves.
The study shows that when students sleep well, their grades go up.

drillQuestions.question: The research shows _____ body language affects judgment.
Their judgments predict _____ or not the doctor will be sued.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 매우 긴 복문으로, shows that → when절 → 주어 → predict → whether절의 구조. 핵심은 "30초 무음 영상만으로도 소송 여부를 예측할 수 있다"는 놀라운 연구 결과이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Nalini Ambady, a researcher at Tufts University
their judgments of the physician's niceness

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 권위에 의한 논증(argument from authority) — 대학 연구자의 연구를 인용하여 주장에 신뢰성 부여

sentences.original: So it doesn't have to do so much with whether or not that physician was incompetent, but do we like that person and how they interacted?
sentences.translation: 그래서 이것은 그 의사가 능력이 없었는지 여부와는 크게 관계가 없고, 우리가 그 사람을 좋아했는지, 어떻게 상호작용했는지가 중요합니다.

grammarElements.text: doesn't have to do so much with
grammarElements.label: 동사구 (부정 — 관계없다)

grammarElements.text: whether or not that physician was incompetent
grammarElements.label: 명사절 (whether)

vocabulary.example: The manager was fired for being incompetent.
Students interact with each other during group work.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것이 특정 요인과 무관함을 나타낼 때

expressions.phrase: not so much X but Y
expressions.usage: 실제 원인이나 핵심을 대조적으로 제시할 때

patterns.pattern: It doesn't have to do so much with [X], but [Y]
patterns.explanation: 진짜 원인이 X가 아니라 Y임을 밝히는 대조 패턴.
examples: It doesn't have to do so much with talent, but with practice.
It doesn't have to do with money, but with passion.

drillQuestions.question: It doesn't have to do so much _____ intelligence, but with effort.

sentences.notes: "have to do with"는 "관련이 있다"는 의미의 숙어. incompetent의 접두사 in-은 부정을 나타내며, competent(유능한)의 반의어이다.

koreanAlignment.en: it doesn't have to do so much with
whether or not that physician was incompetent
but do we like that person

sentences.original: Even more dramatic, Alex Todorov at Princeton has shown us that judgments of political candidates' faces in just one second predict 70 percent of U.S. Senate and gubernatorial race outcomes.
sentences.translation: 더 극적인 것은, 프린스턴의 알렉스 토도로프가 정치 후보자의 얼굴에 대한 단 1초의 판단이 미국 상원과 주지사 선거 결과의 70퍼센트를 예측한다는 것을 보여주었습니다.

grammarElements.text: that judgments of political candidates' faces in just one second predict 70 percent of U.S. Senate and gubernatorial race outcomes
grammarElements.label: that절 (직접목적어)

vocabulary.example: The results were even more dramatic than expected.
Several candidates applied for the position.
She won the gubernatorial election by a large margin.
The outcome of the experiment surprised everyone.

expressions.usage: 이전 예시보다 더 놀라운 사례를 소개할 때 사용하는 전환 표현
매우 짧은 시간을 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: [Researcher] has shown us that [finding]
patterns.explanation: 연구 결과를 소개하는 현재완료 패턴. has shown은 연구가 이미 완료되었지만 현재까지 유효함을 나타냄.
examples: Studies have shown us that sleep deprivation affects memory.
Research has shown us that diversity improves team performance.

drillQuestions.question: Alex Todorov has _____ us that first impressions matter.
_____ of political candidates' faces predict election outcomes.

sentences.notes: gubernatorial은 governor(주지사)의 형용사형으로, 일상에서는 잘 안 쓰이지만 뉴스와 정치 영어에서는 필수. "1초 만에 70%를 예측"이라는 수치가 강연의 핵심 논거이다.

koreanAlignment.en: U.S. Senate and gubernatorial race outcomes

pronunciationNotes: gubernatorial: /ˌɡuːbərnəˈtɔːriəl/ — "구버너토리얼", 강세는 네 번째 음절. 가장 어려운 발음

sentences.original: So when we think of nonverbals, we think of how we judge others, how they judge us and what the outcomes are.
sentences.translation: 비언어적 신호를 생각할 때, 우리는 어떻게 다른 사람을 판단하는지, 그들이 어떻게 우리를 판단하는지, 그리고 그 결과가 무엇인지를 생각합니다.

grammarElements.text: how we judge others, how they judge us and what the outcomes are
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (3개의 간접의문문 병렬)

vocabulary.example: Don't judge a book by its cover.

expressions.usage: think about보다 가볍고 즉각적인 연상을 나타낼 때

patterns.pattern: We think of how [A], how [B], and what [C]
patterns.explanation: 세 가지 간접의문문을 병렬로 나열하는 패턴. 복수의 관점을 효과적으로 정리.
examples: We consider how they learn, how they grow, and what they achieve.
Think about how you start, how you continue, and what you accomplish.

drillQuestions.question: We think of _____ we judge others.

sentences.notes: 세 개의 간접의문문(how we judge, how they judge, what the outcomes are)이 병렬 구조를 이루어 논점을 깔끔하게 요약한다. 이 문장은 앞 내용의 요약이자 다음 내용으로의 전환.

koreanAlignment.en: we think of how we judge others

pronunciationNotes: judge: /dʒʌdʒ/ — "저지", 첫 자음과 마지막 자음이 동일

sentences.original: We tend to forget, though, the other audience that's influenced by our nonverbals, and that's ourselves.
sentences.translation: 하지만 우리는 비언어적 신호에 영향을 받는 또 다른 관객, 바로 우리 자신을 잊는 경향이 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: the other audience that's influenced by our nonverbals
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계대명사절 포함)

vocabulary.example: People tend to eat more when they are stressed.
The speaker connected well with the audience.
Music can influence our mood.

expressions.usage: 사람들이 간과하기 쉬운 것을 지적할 때
극적인 반전을 주는 마무리 표현

patterns.pattern: We tend to forget [something] that's [past participle] by ..., and that's [reveal]
patterns.explanation: 관계대명사 that으로 수식하고, 마지막에 반전(reveal)을 주는 수사적 패턴.
examples: We tend to overlook the person who is most affected by our choices, and that is ourselves.

drillQuestions.question: We tend _____ forget the other audience.
The audience that's _____ by our nonverbals is ourselves.

sentences.notes: 이 문장이 강연의 핵심 전환점. "보디랭귀지가 영향을 미치는 또 다른 관객은 바로 우리 자신"이라는 반전. though가 문장 중간에 삽입되어 양보의 뉘앙스를 더한다. that's는 두 번 나오는데 각각 관계대명사와 지시대명사.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반전(twist) — 청중이 예상하지 못한 "또 다른 관객 = 자기 자신"이라는 메시지로 핵심 논지 전달

sentences.original: We are also influenced by our nonverbals, our thoughts and our feelings and our physiology.
sentences.translation: 우리 또한 우리의 비언어적 신호, 생각, 감정, 그리고 생리 작용에 의해 영향을 받습니다.

grammarElements.text: by our nonverbals, our thoughts and our feelings and our physiology
grammarElements.label: 행위자 (by + 병렬 나열)

vocabulary.example: Understanding physiology helps us take better care of our bodies.

expressions.usage: 어떤 요인이 우리에게 영향을 미칠 때

patterns.pattern: We are also influenced by [A], [B], [C], and [D]
patterns.explanation: 수동태와 병렬 나열을 결합. 여러 영향 요인을 체계적으로 제시.
examples: We are influenced by our culture, our education, and our experiences.

drillQuestions.question: We are also _____ by our nonverbals.

sentences.notes: our가 4번 반복되는 병렬 구조로 "비언어, 생각, 감정, 생리"라는 4가지 영역을 모두 포함한다는 강조. physiology는 학술적 어휘로, 여기서는 호르몬 등의 신체 반응을 의미한다.

sentences.original: So what nonverbals am I talking about? I'm a social psychologist. I study prejudice, and I teach at a competitive business school, so it was inevitable that I would become interested in power dynamics.
sentences.translation: 그렇다면 제가 말하는 비언어적 신호란 무엇일까요? 저는 사회심리학자입니다. 편견을 연구하고, 경쟁이 치열한 비즈니스 스쿨에서 가르치고 있어서, 권력 역학에 관심을 갖게 된 것은 필연적이었습니다.

grammarElements.text: and I teach at a competitive business school
grammarElements.label: 등위절

grammarElements.text: so it was inevitable that I would become interested in power dynamics
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (so + that절)

vocabulary.example: The psychologist helped her deal with anxiety.
We should work to overcome our prejudices.
Change is inevitable in any organization.
The job market is very competitive.
Understanding power dynamics is essential for leadership.

expressions.usage: 결과가 자연스럽게 나올 수밖에 없었음을 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: I [verb X] and I [verb Y], so it was inevitable that I would [verb Z]
patterns.explanation: 두 가지 배경을 제시한 후, 자연스러운 결과(결론)로 이어지는 구조.
examples: I love science and I work in a lab, so it was inevitable that I would pursue a PhD.
She studied economics and worked in finance, so it was inevitable that she would start her own fund.

drillQuestions.question: It was _____ that she would become interested in the topic.
I became especially interested _____ power dynamics.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 화자가 자기 소개를 하면서 연구 주제로 자연스럽게 넘어가는 전환 역할. so it was inevitable that 구조는 논리적 필연성을 나타내는 격식적 표현이다. 세 개의 I 문장이 연속되어 개인적 배경을 전달.

koreanAlignment.en: what nonverbals am I talking about
I teach at a competitive business school
that I would become interested in power dynamics
koreanAlignment.ko: 권력 역학에 관심을 갖게 된 것은
